epigraph.text: Where there is no vision, the people perish.

content.text:
Numbers in a table can be hard to interpret. But when those same numbers 
are displayed as a graph, patterns leap off the page. In this section, 
we learn to read and create two fundamental types of graphs: 
**bar graphs** for comparing categories and **line graphs** 
for showing change over time.

A **bar graph** uses rectangular bars to represent data. The length 
or height of each bar shows the value it represents. Bar graphs are 
ideal for comparing quantities across different categories.

content.title: Bar Graph
content.content:
A **bar graph** displays data using rectangular 
bars where:

- Each bar represents a category or group.

- The length or height of the bar represents the value.

- Bars can be vertical or horizontal.

- Bars should have equal widths and be evenly spaced.

content.title: Reading a bar graph
content.problem:
The bar graph below shows the number of books read by students during 
summer vacation.

center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 ybar,
 width=10cm,
 height=6cm,
 ylabel={Number of Books},
 symbolic x coords={Anna, Ben, Chloe, David, Emma},
 xtick=data,
 ymin=0,
 ymax=12,
 bar width=0.6cm,
 nodes near coords,
 axis lines=left,
 ]
 coordinates {(Anna,7) (Ben,4) (Chloe,10) (David,5) (Emma,8)};
 axis
tikzpicture
center

Answer these questions:

1. Who read the most books?

2. How many more books did Emma read than Ben?

3. What is the total number of books read by all students?
content.solution:
1. **Chloe** read the most books (10).

2. Emma read 8 books, Ben read 4 books. 
 $8 - 4 = \textbf{4}$ more books.

3. $7 + 4 + 10 + 5 + 8 = \textbf{34}$ books total.

content.title: Creating a Bar Graph
content.content:
1. Draw and label the axes. Put categories on one axis and values 
 on the other.

2. Choose a scale for the value axis that fits your data.

3. Draw bars of equal width for each category.

4. Make the height (or length) of each bar match its value.

5. Add a title that describes the data.

content.title: Creating a bar graph
content.problem:
Create a bar graph for this data on favorite sports:

| Sport | Number of Students |
| Basketball | 12 |
| Soccer | 18 |
| Baseball | 9 |
| Swimming | 6 |
content.solution:
center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 ybar,
 width=10cm,
 height=6cm,
 ylabel={Number of Students},
 xlabel={Sport},
 symbolic x coords={Basketball, Soccer, Baseball, Swimming},
 xtick=data,
 ymin=0,
 ymax=22,
 bar width=0.8cm,
 nodes near coords,
 axis lines=left,
 title={Favorite Sports Survey},
 ]
 coordinates {(Basketball,12) (Soccer,18) (Baseball,9) (Swimming,6)};
 axis
tikzpicture
center

The graph clearly shows that soccer is the most popular sport and 
swimming is the least popular among the students surveyed.

content.text:
A **double bar graph** compares two related sets of data side by 
side. This makes it easy to see differences between groups.

content.title: Reading a double bar graph
content.problem:
The double bar graph shows test scores for boys and girls in four subjects.

center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 ybar,
 width=11cm,
 height=6cm,
 ylabel={Average Score},
 symbolic x coords={Math, Science, English, History},
 xtick=data,
 ymin=0,
 ymax=100,
 bar width=0.4cm,
 legend style={at={(0.5,-0.2)}, anchor=north, legend columns=2},
 axis lines=left,
 ]
 coordinates {(Math,85) (Science,82) (English,78) (History,80)};
 coordinates {(Math,82) (Science,80) (English,88) (History,83)};
 Boys, Girls
 axis
tikzpicture
center

1. In which subject did boys score higher than girls?

2. In which subject was the difference between boys and girls 
 greatest?

3. What was the girls' average English score?
content.solution:
1. Boys scored higher in **Math** (85 vs.\ 82) and 
 **Science** (82 vs.\ 80).

2. The greatest difference was in **English**: girls scored 
 88, boys scored 78—a difference of 10 points.

3. Girls' English score: **88**.

content.text:
A **line graph** shows how data changes over time. Points are 
plotted and connected by lines, making trends easy to see.

content.title: Line Graph
content.content:
A **line graph** displays data points connected 
by lines where:

- The horizontal axis typically represents time.

- The vertical axis represents the quantity being measured.

- Points show values at specific times.

- Lines connect the points to show trends.

content.title: Reading a line graph
content.problem:
The line graph shows monthly rainfall in a city.

center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 width=11cm,
 height=6cm,
 ylabel={Rainfall (inches)},
 xlabel={Month},
 symbolic x coords={Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct, Nov, Dec},
 xtick=data,
 ymin=0,
 ymax=6,
 axis lines=left,
 mark=*,
 x tick label style={rotate=45, anchor=east},
 ]
 coordinates {(Jan,2.5) (Feb,2.8) (Mar,3.5) (Apr,4.2) (May,4.8) 
 (Jun,3.2) (Jul,1.8) (Aug,1.5) (Sep,2.0) (Oct,2.8) (Nov,3.2) (Dec,2.6)};
 axis
tikzpicture
center

1. Which month had the most rainfall?

2. Which month had the least rainfall?

3. Describe the overall trend from January to May.
content.solution:
1. **May** had the most rainfall (4.8 inches).

2. **August** had the least rainfall (1.5 inches).

3. Rainfall **increased** from January (2.5 in.) to 
 May (4.8 in.).

content.title: Creating a Line Graph
content.content:
1. Draw and label the axes. Time usually goes on the horizontal axis.

2. Choose appropriate scales for both axes.

3. Plot a point for each data value.

4. Connect the points with straight lines.

5. Add a title and label the axes.

content.title: Creating a line graph
content.problem:
A student tracked daily study time (in minutes) for a week:

| Day | Mon | Tue | Wed | Thu | Fri | Sat | Sun |
| Minutes | 30 | 45 | 40 | 55 | 25 | 60 | 50 |
content.solution:
center
tikzpicture
 axis[
 width=10cm,
 height=6cm,
 ylabel={Minutes Studied},
 xlabel={Day of Week},
 symbolic x coords={Mon, Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun},
 xtick=data,
 ymin=0,
 ymax=70,
 axis lines=left,
 mark=*,
 title={Daily Study Time},
 ]
 coordinates {(Mon,30) (Tue,45) (Wed,40) (Thu,55) (Fri,25) (Sat,60) (Sun,50)};
 axis
tikzpicture
center

From the graph, we can see that study time varied throughout the 
week, with the most time spent on Saturday and the least on Friday.

content.title: Choosing a graph type
content.problem:
Which type of graph would be best?

1. Comparing sales by different stores

2. Showing temperature changes during a day

3. Displaying favorite ice cream flavors

4. Tracking a student's grades over a semester
content.solution:
1. **Bar graph**—comparing categories (stores)

2. **Line graph**—showing change over time

3. **Bar graph**—comparing categories (flavors)

4. **Line graph**—tracking change over time

content.title: William Playfair: Inventor of Graphs
content.content:
The bar graph and line graph were invented by Scottish engineer 
William Playfair in the late 1700s. Before Playfair, data was 
presented only in tables. Playfair realized that visual 
representations could make patterns immediately apparent. His 
1786 book, *The Commercial and Political Atlas*, contained 
the first bar chart and line graph ever published.

marginNotes.text:
The heavens declare the glory of God; the 
skies proclaim the work of his hands.

There is a time for everything, and 
a season for every activity under the heavens.

marginNotes.author: Edward Tufte
marginNotes.text:
The commonality between science and art is 
in trying to see profoundly.
